content_id:
Al-Mughni secara formal adalah syarah atas Mukhtasar al-Khiraqi, sebuah teks singkat namun mendasar dari fikih Hanbali awal yang digubah oleh Umar ibn al-Husayn al-Khiraqi pada abad keempat H. Mukhtasar adalah ringkasan ringkas dari pendapat-pendapat hukum Hanbali, merekam pendapat-pendapat yang ditransmisikan dari Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal dan generasi awal murid-muridnya. Ibn Qudamah menggunakan kerangka ini sebagai titik awal, tetapi syarahnya melampaui teks dasar begitu besar dalam cakupan dan kedalaman sehingga Al-Mughni menjadi ensiklopedia independen daripada sekadar penjelasan atas Mukhtasar.

Metodologi Al-Mughni bersifat komparatif dan berlatar hadis. Untuk setiap pertanyaan hukum, Ibn Qudamah dimulai dengan menyatakan pendapat Hanbali, kemudian mensurvei pendapat-pendapat tiga mazhab besar lainnya -- Hanafi, Maliki, dan Syafi'i -- beserta pandangan-pandangan para Sahabat dan Tabi'in. Ia kemudian mengajukan dalil tekstual pendukung: ayat-ayat Al-Quran, hadis, konsensus, dan analogi hukum. Ketika ulama berbeda pendapat, ia mengevaluasi kekuatan dalil setiap pendapat dan sering mempertahankan pandangan Hanbali sambil mengakui penalaran yang berbeda.

Metode komparatif ini menjadikan Al-Mughni jauh lebih dari sekadar manual satu mazhab. Ini adalah karya usul -- penalaran hukum dari sumber-sumber primer -- yang tertanam dalam syarah fikih. Para pelajar dan ulama yang menggunakan buku ini belajar tidak hanya apa yang dikatakan kaum Hanbali tetapi bagaimana argumen-argumen hukum dibangun, bagaimana hadis-hadis ditimbang satu sama lain, dan bagaimana konsensus ditetapkan atau diperdebatkan. Pelatihan dalam metodologi ini telah menjadikan Al-Mughni berharga bagi para ulama di seluruh mazhab.

Karya ini disusun dalam urutan standar fikih Islam: taharah, shalat, zakat, puasa, haji, jihad, kemudian muamalah, hukum keluarga, dan hukum pidana. Sepuluh jilid dari edisi standar mencerminkan cakupan komprehensif ini. Setiap bab utama sendiri merupakan treatment yang lengkap atas topiknya, cukup menyeluruh untuk berdiri sendiri sebagai rujukan.
content_en:
Al-Mughni is formally a commentary on Mukhtasar al-Khiraqi, a brief but foundational text of early Hanbali jurisprudence composed by Umar ibn al-Husayn al-Khiraqi in the fourth century AH. The Mukhtasar is a terse summary of Hanbali legal positions, recording the opinions transmitted from Imam Ahmad ibn Hanbal and the early generation of his students. Ibn Qudamah used this framework as a starting point, but his commentary so vastly exceeded the base text in scope and depth that al-Mughni became an independent encyclopedia rather than merely an explanation of the Mukhtasar.

The methodology of al-Mughni is comparative and hadith-grounded. For each legal question, Ibn Qudamah begins by stating the Hanbali position, then surveys the positions of the other three major schools — Hanafi, Maliki, and Shafi'i — along with the views of the Companions and Successors. He then adduces the supporting textual evidence: Quranic verses, hadith, consensus, and legal analogy. When scholars disagree, he evaluates the strength of each position's evidence and often defends the Hanbali view while acknowledging the reasoning of the dissenters.

This comparative method makes al-Mughni far more than a single-madhab manual. It is a work of usul — of legal reasoning from primary sources — embedded within a fiqh commentary. Students and scholars using the book learn not just what Hanbalis say but how legal arguments are constructed, how hadith are weighed against one another, and how consensus is established or disputed. This training in methodology has made al-Mughni valuable to scholars across the madhhabs.

The work is organized in the standard sequence of Islamic jurisprudence: tahara, salah, zakah, sawm, hajj, jihad, then transactions, family law, and criminal law. The ten volumes of the standard edition reflect this comprehensive scope. Each major chapter is itself a complete treatment of its topic, thorough enough to stand alone as a reference.
content_ar: Al-Mughni is formally a commentary on Mukhtasar al-Khiraqi, a brief but foundational text of early الحنبلي jurisprudence composed by عمر ibn al-Husayn al-Khiraqi in the رابعاً century AH. The Mukhtasar is a terse summary of الحنبلي legal positions, recording the opinions transmitted from الإمام أحمد ابن حنبل and the early generation of his students. Ibn Qudamah used this framework as a starting point, but his commentary لذا vastly exceeded the base text in scope and depth that al-Mughni became an independent encyclopedia rather than merely an explanation of the Mukhtasar. The methodology of al-Mughni is comparative and الحديث-grounded. For each legal question, Ibn Qudamah begins by stating the الحنبلي position, then surveys the positions of the other three major schools — الحنفي, المالكي, and الشافعي — along with the views of the الصحابة and التابعين. He then adduces the supporting textual evidence: القرآني الآيات, الحديث, consensus, and legal analogy. عندما العلماء disagree, he evaluates the القوة of each position's evidence and often defends the الحنبلي view بينما acknowledging the reasoning of the dissenters. This comparative method makes al-Mughni far more than a single-madhab manual. It is a work of الأصول — of legal reasoning from الأساسي sources — embedded within a الفقه commentary. Students and العلماء using the الكتاب learn not just what Hanbalis say but how legal arguments are constructed, how الحديث are weighed against بعضهم بعضاً, and how consensus is established or disputed. This training in methodology has made al-Mughni valuable to العلماء across the madhhabs. The work is organized in the standard sequence of Islamic jurisprudence: tahara, الصلاة, الزكاة, الصيام, الحج, jihad, then transactions, الأسرة law, and criminal law. The ten volumes of the standard edition reflect this comprehensive scope. Each major الباب is itself a complete treatment of its topic, thorough enough to stand alone as a reference.
